skill.name: sequence-alignment
skill.description: A skill for performing sequence alignment using NCBI BLAST API. Supports nucleotide and protein sequence comparison against major biological databases.
skill.summary: Run BLAST sequence alignment searches against NCBI biological databases for DNA and protein analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 110 potential issues (27 high, 55 medium, 28 low). All high and medium findings are false positives - 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags refer to the BLAST bioinformatics algorithm (not cryptography), 'system reconnaissance' flags are bioinformatics documentation, 'path traversal' is a security checklist item, and 'shell backtick execution' flags are markdown code blocks. The only legitimate findings are standard HTTP calls to NCBI BLAST API (via urllib) and file write operations, which are the core expected functionality of this bioinformatics skill. No malicious intent or data exfiltration detected.

high_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm reference (false positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'BLAST' as a weak cryptographic algorithm. BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) is a bioinformatics sequence alignment algorithm, not a cryptographic function. All occurrences are legitimate references in bioinformatics documentation and code comments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations refer to the BLAST bioinformatics algorithm or use the word 'description' in argparse setup. The static scanner incorrectly matched the substring 'BLAST' against weak crypto patterns.

high_findings.title: System reconnaissance indicators (false positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged comments and documentation about BLAST programs, status codes, and database names as system reconnaissance. These are standard bioinformatics terminology in documentation and code, not hostile reconnaissance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations contain standard bioinformatics documentation terms and descriptive variable names. None indicate hostile reconnaissance behavior.

high_findings.title: Path traversal sequence (false positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged '../' in a security checklist item. SKILL.md line 165 is a checklist entry that says 'No unauthorized file system access' as a security best practice to follow, not an actual path traversal attempt.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is part of a security compliance checklist that instructs users to avoid path traversal. It is explicitly documenting a security control, not implementing one.

medium_findings.title: Shell backtick execution in documentation (false positive)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick-enclosed text in markdown files as shell command execution. These are markdown code blocks and inline code formatting in documentation files (references/ncbi_api_guide.md) and SKILL.md showing example bash commands and API parameters. No actual command execution occurs from these documentation files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged text is inside markdown code fences or inline backtick formatting. No shell execution mechanism exists - these are documentation examples for user reference only.

low_findings.title: HTTP network calls to NCBI BLAST API (legitimate)
low_findings.description: The skill uses Python urllib to make POST requests to the NCBI BLAST API at https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi. This is the core functionality of the skill - submitting sequences for alignment and retrieving results. No unauthorized or suspicious endpoints are contacted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network calls target the official NCBI BLAST endpoint using standard HTTPS. No credential exfiltration, encoded payloads, or suspicious destinations detected.

low_findings.title: File write for BLAST results output (legitimate)
low_findings.description: The skill writes BLAST alignment results to a user-specified output file path using Python's open(). This is an expected feature for saving analysis results. The output path is provided by the user via the --output CLI argument.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write uses a user-provided output path with standard open(). No path traversal, no hardcoded paths, and no overwriting of system files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded NCBI BLAST URLs in documentation
low_findings.description: References to NCBI websites and API endpoints in documentation files. These are legitimate citations and API documentation URLs for the BLAST service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to public NCBI websites and documentation resources. No suspicious or unknown domains detected.

content.user_title: Run BLAST Sequence Alignments on NCBI Databases
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics researchers need to compare unknown DNA or protein sequences against known databases but lack a streamlined tool. This skill submits sequences to the NCBI BLAST API and returns formatted alignment results with scores, e-values, and visual match highlighting.
actual_capabilities: Submits nucleotide (DNA) sequences to BLAST against nt, est, gss, and refseq_rna databases
Submits protein sequences to BLAST against nr, swissprot, pdb, and refseq_protein databases
Supports five BLAST programs: blastn, blastp, blastx, tblastn, and tblastx
Returns formatted results in text, JSON, or CSV format with scores and e-values
Displays alignment visualization with query, subject, and match/mismatch highlighting
Saves results to a user-specified output file when requested
limitations: Requires internet access to reach NCBI BLAST API at blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov
Limited to NCBI-hosted databases only; does not support local BLAST installations
Search speed depends on NCBI server load and queue wait time (RTOE)
Does not support batch submission of multiple sequences in a single request

use_cases.title: Identify Unknown DNA Sequences
use_cases.description: A researcher has sequenced a DNA sample and needs to identify the organism or gene it came from. This skill runs blastn against the nt database to find matching sequences.
use_cases.target_user: Molecular biologist with unknown sequences from lab experiments

use_cases.title: Find Protein Homologs Across Species
use_cases.description: A computational biologist has a protein of interest and wants to find similar proteins in other organisms. The skill runs blastp against the nr or swissprot database to find homologs.

use_cases.title: Annotate Predicted Gene Sequences
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics student has predicted gene sequences from genome assembly and needs functional annotation. The skill runs blastx to translate and compare nucleotides against protein databases.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics student in a genome annotation course

prompt_templates.title: Basic DNA Sequence Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a BLAST search on this DNA sequence: ATGCGTACGTAGCTAGCTAG. Use blastn against the nt database and show the top 5 hits in text format.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick identification of an unknown DNA sequence

prompt_templates.title: High-Confidence Protein Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for homologous proteins matching this sequence: MKTAYIAKQRQISFVKSHFSRQLEERLGLIEVQAPILSRVGDGT. Use blastp against the swissprot database with an e-value threshold of 0.001 and return results as JSON.
prompt_templates.scenario: Finding high-confidence protein homologs with strict significance threshold

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Species Translated Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Take this nucleotide sequence from a metagenomics sample: ATGGCCCTGTGGATGCGCTTCTTAGTCG. Run blastx against the nr database and explain what proteins the translated sequence matches.
prompt_templates.scenario: Identifying potential protein coding regions in unknown DNA

prompt_templates.title: Full Pipeline with File Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a comprehensive BLAST analysis on this sequence: ATGCGTACGTAGCTAGCTAGCATG. Use blastn against the nt database with evalue 0.01 and max hits 20. Save results to blast_results.csv and summarize the top hits.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complete analysis workflow with file export for downstream processing

output_examples.input: Run BLAST search on DNA sequence ATGCGTACGTAGCTAGCTAG using blastn against nt database
output: Definition: Homo sapiens hypothetical gene sequence

output_examples.input: Search for similar proteins using blastp against swissprot with evalue 0.001
output: Top matches found in swissprot database:
- Cytochrome c oxidase subunit 1 (Score: 1200, E-value: 3.4e-60, 95% identity)
- Cytochrome c oxidase subunit 2 (Score: 850, E-value: 2.1e-40, 88% identity)
All hits have e-values below the 0.001 threshold, indicating high statistical significance.
The top hit shows strong conservation across the full alignment length.

best_practices: Start with a high e-value threshold (0.001) for high-confidence searches, then relax the threshold if no significant hits are found
Match the BLAST program to your sequence type: blastn for DNA vs DNA, blastp for protein vs protein, blastx for translated DNA vs protein
Use the JSON output format when processing results programmatically and text format for human review
anti_patterns: Do not submit raw sequencing reads (long or low-quality) as queries; use assembled contigs or consensus sequences instead
Do not ignore the RTOE (estimated wait time); BLAST searches can take minutes and the skill polls for results with a 5-minute timeout
Do not use blastp on nucleotide sequences or blastn on protein sequences; this will cause an API error from NCBI

faq.question: What databases can I search with this skill?
faq.answer: The skill supports nr (non-redundant proteins), nt (nucleotide collection), swissprot (curated proteins), pdb (Protein Data Bank), refseq_protein, refseq_rna, est (expressed sequence tags), and gss (genome survey sequences).

faq.question: How long does a BLAST search usually take?
faq.answer: The NCBI server returns an estimated wait time (RTOE) in seconds. Short sequences typically take 10-30 seconds. The skill polls for results with a maximum 5-minute timeout.

faq.question: Can I run multiple sequences at once?
faq.answer: No, the current version only supports one sequence per request. You can run multiple requests sequentially for batch analysis.

faq.question: Does this skill work offline?
faq.answer: No, an active internet connection is required because the skill sends queries to the NCBI BLAST API server.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Three formats are available: text (human-readable with alignment visualization), JSON (structured data for programming), and CSV (tabular format for spreadsheets).

faq.question: Is there a limit on sequence length?
faq.answer: NCBI BLAST accepts sequences up to several thousand base pairs, but very long sequences may time out with the 5-minute maximum wait time. For sequences over 1000 bases, a high e-value threshold is recommended.
